text: The surgeon performs a pancreaticoduodenectomy using five ports strategically arranged for upper abdominal surgery.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac, revealing the inferior mesenteric vein located below the border of the pancreas.

The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric vein beneath the neck of the pancreas, revealing the confluence of veins from the middle colic and pancreatic side joining the superior mesenteric vein, serving as a landmark for further identification.

The surgeon performs an aggressive and extensive mobilization of the hepatic flexure during a laparoscopic pancreaticoduodenectomy, flipping the right colon over to the left side to facilitate access to the duodenum.
The surgeon aggressively mobilizes the duodenum, freeing it from the retroperitoneum to expose the superior mesenteric portal vein axis and the ansenite process, which are central to the procedure.
The surgeon exposes both the inferior vena cava and the aorta, extending the exposure up to the left border of the aorta, ensuring visibility beyond just the anterior surface.
This is very important because it will ease up the later resection of the antenatal process.

The surgeon removes pancreatic and peri-duodenal lymph nodes along with the specimen, demonstrating the peeling of the mesocolon root away from the duodenum and uncinate process until the duodenum, retroperitoneum, and mesocolon are fully mobilized.
The surgeon achieves a wide view of the superior mesenteric portal vein axis, crucial for the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The inferior vena cava becomes visible as the surgeon continues mobilizing the duodenum from both the right and left sides of the vessel.

The surgeon aggressively exposes the left renal vein and continues peeling the mesocolon away from the retroperitoneum, duodenum, and uncinate process. The aorta is exposed, and visible lymph nodes are identified for later removal as part of the radical procedure.

The surgeon connects the plane of the superior mesenteric vein with the retroperitoneal plane, revealing tributaries from the pancreas to the superior mesenteric vein. These tributaries are visible and require careful control to prevent bleeding.
Veins can be very annoying during surgery and can waste a lot of time.
The surgeon removes the lymph nodes located in the para-aortic area, which were previously identified, as part of the specimen for radical resection.
The junction between the superior mesenteric vein plane and the retroperitoneal plane is clearly visible, with remaining adhesions being gradually dissected to connect the two planes.
The surgeon exposes the duodenojejunal junction (DJ) and the adjacent small bowel, revealing the anatomical structures on the opposite side.
The surgeon proceeds with the suprapancreatic dissection, targeting lymph nodes located in the common hepatic and hepatic regions.
The surgeon exposes the gastrointestinal artery for ligation and reveals the supra-pancreatic portal vein, highlighting these structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects and removes lymph nodes located near the hepatic region, with the portal vein visible above the pancreas.
The surgeon has completed the dissection of the lymphatics, revealing the origin of the gastroduodenal artery and a segment of the portal vein situated between the hepatic artery and the pancreas.
The surgeon cuts the gastroduodenal artery between clips, with the surrounding anatomical structures visible, including the portal vein and pancreas.

The surgeon proceeds with the preliminary resection, revealing the portal vein clearly after cutting the gastrointestinal arc. An instrument is passed from below the pancreas to the dissected area above, and a tape is inserted around the neck of the pancreas for traction.
The surgical field is fully exposed, revealing the duodenum, the head of the pancreas, the uncinate process, the superior mesenteric vein, and the portal vein.
The surgeon begins the resection by cutting the stomach and pancreas, followed by the jejunum and the common bile duct, using surgical instruments to separate these structures.
The surgeon carefully staples the pancreas, applying gradual compression to avoid fracturing the pancreatic parenchyma.
The surgeon applies gentle, gradual compression while stapling the pancreas to avoid fracturing the pancreatic parenchyma.
The surgeon secures and ligates major venous tributaries visible in the surgical field before proceeding with further dissection.
The surgeon takes down the gastrocolic trunk of Henle, then proceeds to separate the vein from the ansenite process.

The surgeon transects the jejunum and passes the transected segment below the vessels to the right side, positioning the specimen on one side. The resection of the uncinate process is initiated, followed by the cutting of the common bile duct to complete the resection.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the superior mesenteric artery, removing tissues surrounding the artery at the retroperitoneal margin of the uncinate process, ensuring the dissection is performed in the proper anatomical plane.
to ensure a negative margin after whipping
The surgeon continues the dissection upwards towards the common bile duct (CBD), carefully separating surrounding tissues to expose the duct.
The surgeon aims to suspend the entire specimen by the common bile duct (CBD) and plans to cut the CBD as the final step of the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery and a harmonic scalpel to progressively dissect tissue along the proper margin, ensuring precise separation of anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the ansenite process, ensuring not to encroach on it, as the dissection progresses towards the common bile duct (CBD).

An aberrant right hepatic artery is visible, originating from the superior mesenteric artery and passing behind the portal vein, closely approaching the common bile duct (CBD). The surgeon carefully navigates these anatomical anomalies before cutting the CBD as the final step in the resection.
The surgical field shows the area after the completion of the resection, with the cut common bile duct (CBD) visible as the final step in the procedure.
The surgeon begins the reconstruction phase by creating a hepatico-jejunostomy, connecting the hepatic duct to the jejunum.
A gelatin catheter stent is inserted through the abdominal wall into the jejunum due to the normal caliber of the common bile duct (CBD).

The surgeon inserts a stent into the hepatic ducts to support the anastomosis due to the small caliber of the common bile duct (CBD). A purse-string suture is placed around the entry point, followed by the anastomosis using a running suture with PDS-C-O.
The surgeon completes the closure of the remaining walls of the hepatico-jejunostomy anastomosis, ensuring the surgical site is secure.
The surgeon proceeds with the gastro-jejunostomy, displaying the specimen with adequate margins and bulky lymph node areas. The ulcerated duodenum and the opened head of the pancreas reveal the mass.
